speaker: interviewer
text: --Libman. Faigie, we were talking about the ghetto, and that it-- you told us that it was situated in Slabodka. Was this the site of the famous yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the yeshiva and other centers of learning in this region?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the Slabodka, it was one of the very famous ones-- famous ones in Lithuania. And maybe that's why they picked it to have a ghetto to there. Naturally, you weren't allowed to study. You weren't studying anymore. They were all-- everything was confiscated. But the belief in learning-- I remember vividly going with friends somewhere in a house. Somebody was playing outside and watching-- if any soldiers, or any Lithuanian soldiers, or Nazis would come by and see that we were learning or studying-- because we were. We were learning Yiddish. We were learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it dangerous to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were caught what would have happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you were caught-- I don't know. We would have probably killed-- be killed. It was very dangerous. It was only on their permission you were allowed to have a little concert-- like I said before, that concert-- only under their permission. They wanted nothing. We shouldn't know anything. We shouldn't learn. We used to, through the grapevine, learn what was happening somewheres else. And the children, especially the children, were taught to believe it's going to be all right. We must learn and we must study. And I suppose this thread is brought through my whole life, that studying and learning is very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking now 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about what was happening to the Polish Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard that-- we heard about concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: From who?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. People probably snuck in. I don't know. We heard that we were being killed, all over, and there is nobody to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you account for your safety in Lithuania? You were in a ghetto. There were no major deportations ongoing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 44. Lucky-- luck-- all luck-- and I suppose my mother, and my father, and luck; our morale was still good. We were together-- bigger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your mother's parents, what had happened to them during this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: What had happened to them? We didn't find out till later on. You have to understand that they were killed. All the outlying small towns were annihilated. In the beginning--

speaker: interviewer
text: We are talking what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, '42 or maybe earlier. They-- some ran away. Some hid. Some-- very few Lithuanians took in. I say very few. And they were the first ones killed-- completely. The synagogues were destroyed. Everything was destroyed-- the outlying small villages, small towns. Krekenava and Panevezys were very cosmopolitan too considering how other little villages were horse and buggy. But they were-- immediately. My mother had-- my mother's-- my mother was the [NON-ENGLISH]. It means the youngest one. She had her brother. She had a brother that she doesn't know it all. He left Lithuania for South Africa. All the Jews that are in South Africa are-- have a Lithuanian background. It might be even an ounce, but it has-- Lithuanian Jews went to South Africa. Some went to-- to the States, but mostly went to South Africa. Her brother went to South Africa. Now that was the only boy. She had a sister, Aunt [NON-ENGLISH], who went to New Rochelle. And the one that took us over after the war, [NON-ENGLISH] went to Montreal. Now she also had her oldest sister, [NON-ENGLISH], with five sons killed. Then she had a younger sister [NON-ENGLISH], killed, with four gorgeous children.

speaker: interviewer
text: But during the war, you didn't know this-- this was only--

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war we didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what you found out afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: We only-- after the war. We didn't know anything. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Complete contact was severed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- with-- contact was severed, but we knew that people were killed. We knew that people were dying. We knew where people were taking to concentration camps. But we didn't know any details. We thought that still, you had a big family, that somebody would survive. See, and that's the way life went on. People went out to work. People were lucky. And I think that the worst part was that you didn't know what will happen tomorrow. I think that was the uncertainty-- that you had to live for today. Maybe some people didn't act very nicely, because they didn't-- everybody wanted to live for today.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you if thought that you were gonna outlive this experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I thought, yes-- my mother not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the words that she used to express this to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was always-- she was always afraid. She always wanted to know where I am in her-- to see where I am, or where my father is. Because she was terribly afraid, going through already, seeing what was happening. I, as a young kid, I still had-- I always-- it's a nature. It stays with you forever. You don't-- it doesn't leave you. I always said it's going to be good. I always had hope. But hearing all the things and hearing all the stories, and-- but I was still, again, I had my mother and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the relationship between you and your mother improve a lot--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was closer to her. I started to become closer to her. The war made me closer to my mother. We were together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was the hero. She used to go and bring food and everything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And things continued?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things continued in a very haphazard, not knowing what going-- what's going to be tomorrow. Some lived with hope. Some didn't. Some-- we all-- nobody knew what's gonna happen. But some tried very hard to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know at the time-- I appreciate you were a child-- did you hear of people escaping out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know. If people did it was very hush-hush. And whoever was brought into the ghetto was hush-hush. And like, they disappeared or whatever-- story was told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Between that big selection in October 1943-- and the liquidation--

speaker: interviewee
text: In '41. The selection was in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was another in '43 that you mentioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: '43 when we went-- oh, that was the children going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Until the liquidation of the ghetto, were there ongoing deportations, selections--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quiet ones, like-- promised for better quarters, promises to work-- some people went quietly. Some people didn't. There were selections, but very-- it was on a very small scale.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I witnessed people just going away. And I didn't know if they-- if they will be coming back. I knew my father came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any random acts of atrocity in the ghetto that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --witnessed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially the Nazis. If they didn't like you, the way you walked, you could have been beaten to death. We were scared, because you didn't know. Like I said before, if you walked on the sidewalk, and if he didn't like you-- and especially people dreaded when Rauca came by, because him and his whip-- and his-- was whistling. To this day, I have one pet peeve. I can't stand somebody whistling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because he was always whistling. And I remember being hit over the head by him. You know, he-- that was-- that I suppose that was his pleasure. He enjoyed. And whenever he walked around, I think people disappeared to be out of his reach. Because then we knew what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What happened in July, 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it as you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --saw things happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It started earlier that people-- we heard that the Germans were losing. And we heard that we will have to-- it was-- we were told-- and actually proclamations again-- that we are being moved. The ghetto is being liquidated. And we understood right away that this is-- that the allies-- that somebody was beating the Germans. They weren't any more in control. Now, we were told again to take with-- only that we could carry. And there was no more clothes to change. I remember there is-- we took pictures with-- just mementos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- were there still Jewish leaders--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Kovno--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yes, they were-- of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were relating--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: --these orders to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- a Jewish administration. They had a terrible job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they had a terrible job, because they were told to do-- and they didn't know how those doing would come out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were told to pack only what we can carry, and we're being-- that the ghetto is being liquidated. Naturally, we feared the worst, because by that time, as I said, we knew. We heard of concentration camps. You'd be surprised without even telephones and things, how people heard that we were being gassed. What did you take with you? What did you carry with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, a little suitcase I had, because I didn't have my doll anymore; maybe a little blanket, photos, a few books. And we were put into cattle cars-- in the cattle cars, each family, like together. And we traveled. I don't remember how many days. I remember people fainting. I remember people passing out in the cars, because they gave us some water. There was nowhere to go to the bathroom. There was only a pail. We survived. As I said, my father was young. My mother was young. And I was young. And I don't even remember us giving-- them giving us to eat-- maybe a piece of stale bread. And then we were brought not far from Stutthof. This is when everybody was forced out of the cars-- the cattle cars. And this was July, 1944, where the men were separated from the women.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that journey take from Kovno to Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember-- three, four days? I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Without food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Without food. I remember the water-- water-- a little bit of water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever cry? You sound like you were a good child. What was your feeling in-- in-- in the-- in the wagons?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember being very scared-- very scared. I think that was my scariest time. Because as I said, when they had the big action, I didn't know what was happening. I was still strong. I was still-- I had eaten. I was still good looking. And here it was already '44 that I went through a lot. I remember being very, very scared, because I saw people in the car-- in the car with me fainting and being left there to die. Because everybody was marched out, and the people who were left were left. And I think that was the end emotionally of my father. Because everybody, again, the men was taken-- that the men are going to be sent to work. And the women were going to be killed. They were gonna be sent to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there a station at Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a station. There was like a big, big, big open place again. And the men--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: A selection-- not selection. The men were told to go to one side and the women and children go to another side. And a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was Nazis, Lithuanian--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: The more-- most Nazis-- most Nazis-- not Lithuanians. I don't think so. That was already the Nazis. And that I think was the seed that was planted in my mother's head to make me [NON-ENGLISH], to make me look older. In 1944, I think I was about the same height. I was very nicely built-- flat like a board, but still very nicely built. I didn't grow much more later on. And my father-- naturally, we said-- the-- the goodbyes were horrendous. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: --broke-- broke my heart. I kept on clinging to him. And somebody pulled me away from my father. I remember being pulled away. Because I lost my-- you have to understand. But he was really my best, best, bestest friend, my father. So that was-- but again, I had my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were his last words to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Be strong. And he lost the will to live. Because later on, we found out he gave up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he sent to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Dachau-- all the men, with Elly Gotz-- all the men were sent to Dachau-- and his father too-- Elly Gotz's father too. He told me that afterwards. They were sent to Dachau. All the men were working. Friends of my father, after the war, told me he gave-- he literally gave up, because he was a very nicely built, strong man. But they were sure that we we're going to be killed. What do they-- he saw what happened to his mother, that all the children went to Auschwitz. He saw, so naturally, the women and the children are going to be killed right away. And that when we were sent to Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like we went-- marched into Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your impression of the camp? Was it a concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: A concentration-- Stutthof was a big concentration camp. Again, luck-- again, another miracle-- the ovens, in 1944, in July, the ovens were not working at Stutthof. My first impression was mounds and mountains of shoes. To this day I can't-- my shoes always have to be straight on the ground. There couldn't be two pair of shoes on top of each other. Because that was the first impression I had of Stutthof-- mountains and mountains of shoes, and mountains and mountains of bric-a-brac, like glasses, or combs, or stuff like that. Now we were marched into Stutthof. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Germans didn't know-- like, I mean, don't forget. The Germans kept a very detailed record. But by that time-- people dying in the cattle cars, people-- not everybody. The sick ones were left over in the ghetto. They were killed. It started anew, the registration. Like your name, and who you are, and everything. We were completely disrobed. The women-- I suppose they were looking for gold and for diamonds-- were examined. I remember being sent to a wall-- completely flat like a board. And when they asked your name, instead of-- my mother gave, naturally, their name. Instead of saying that I am 10, she told them I am 12, because they would have seen how they registered me flat like a board. But as soon as we were out of the examining room and showers, we were given clothes. We were not given prisoners clothes. In-- when we meant-- went to Stutthof, I saw women completely shaved and in prisoners clothes. There where a lot of Hungarian Jews in Stutthof-- tremendous amount. They were-- and they had numbers. They didn't have time. You see, we got there in July of '44. They didn't have time to do all those things. When my mother went to pick up clothes-- I remember as today-- she took a brown little jacket that didn't look for a little girl. It looked more--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mature.

speaker: interviewee
text: --more mature; a skirt and clumpen, like shoes that would make high-- taller a little bit, and a bra. And I remember telling her, what do I need a bra? Like I mean, look at me.

speaker: interviewer
text: She stuffed my bra with paper, and she told them that I'm 12 years old. 12 years old is a mature kid. You know what I mean? 12 years old, because she had the feeling that the children, again, are going to be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to Stutthof. And we slept in barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go through showers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Showers before, before we got dressed-- to showers. And again, my mother was a nurse. And they asked-- they-- she helped out in the infirmary. If it was bad in the ghetto, then Stutthof was worse. In the ghetto at least we had our own little place, even a cubicle. There were barracks. And you slept on top of each other. If somebody missed the washroom, there were pails that women, during the night-- were take-- some-- a detail to-- to empty it out in the morning. I-- and the bathrooms, during the day, everybody went together. That I remember too. It was horrendous. The food was terrible. People were very sick. And it was a very downer, even from the ghetto, because here you didn't have anything of your own. I didn't have even a stitch of a paper, or-- or a picture. Everything was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Upon arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Upon arrival, everything-- there was nothing left of your own. You were completely stripped of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think a lot about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But again, I told my mother, if we are okay, he is OK for sure. Because he was a young man. Now again, now comes another miracle. I am full of miracles. My mother hated Stutthof. She had this feeling that in case the ovens were-- don't forget we didn't know the ovens couldn't start again. It wasn't working. We were all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people talk-- the people that had been there longer?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people talked. Of course. They said-- they told about the crematorium. They told-- they showed. You could see the ovens. They weren't just working You can see the mounds of shoes. You can see the mounds of clothing that we picked to put on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they asked, when there was a selection again, to go and dig ditches in small labor camps, my mother volunteered with me again. And I looked very grown up. I had a bust. I was higher-- my heel-- where I had clumpers. They were a little bit higher.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was the nurse. They needed a nurse. So we were picked to go to dig ditches. We left Stutthof. A lot of women-- I don't remember how many. My mother says there was about 1,200 who were picked to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I was in Stutthof in July. In-- we got there in July. We must have been July, August, September. Because when we got to the first small labor camp, that we lived in tents and straw on the floor-- it was still all right. Later, it froze everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was still not too bad, because you slept on straw. It was dry. So we must have been in Stutthof three months-- July, August, September-- maybe the beginning of October. And the name of the first labor camp which you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- I have to-- I have it-- yes. It was Trunz. There were three-- Trunz, Merzen, and Leibisch. We were transported from one after you finished. And let me tell you. I was a terrific worker. We all had-- we didn't have numbers here. But naturally, on our clothes was sown on a number, on your chest. And I was a terrific worker. I used to dig the ditches, because also my mother was in the infirmary. Maybe I had an extra ration. Maybe I had an extra-- she used to take off her jacket and give it to me when it was cold. But this was the beginning. And I got extra rations of soup. The-- we used to get maybe stale bread, but I remember the soup. And I used to share it. Naturally, I lost my friend, Chaya. And I met a lot of people who were in Stutthof and who-- who came with us to the labor camps. And I made a lot of friends, because I was an only child. And I used to share. There is a friend, Gitele, that still-- she is now in the States. When she was in Montreal, used to tell me how much I helped her. I gave her some of my food-- of my soup. Because I used to get an extra ration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she more or less the same age as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Older-- I think a couple years older. And she was alone there. Her parents were killed. She came from a smaller town of-- in Lithuania. She does not come-- she was only-- she was only afterwards in Kovna. She lost all her parents. She lost everybody. So her and I became very close friends. And again, more people came. Again, another miracle-- the miracle was this. That when more women came from Stutthof to work-- because don't forget, more were send again. All, as it says [LAUGHS] in the book, they were-- labor camps were sprung up around Stutthof. They needed the working detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were told again that trucks run the-- roll-- rolled into Stutthof. And all the children and people who were sick, were liquidated. So you see how many miracles I went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we worked. And I was very happy to work. And then it was-- we were transferred to another camp, probably Merzen or Trunz. And I remember the winters, because it was frozen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was going to ask you about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was frozen. The ground was frozen. It was horrible. Your-- your shoes were falling apart. Your-- if it was bad during the nice whether, it was worse during the winter. Because on the straw, in the-- in Yiddish it's called [YIDDISH]-- tents, we lived in tents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given utensils to work with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to dig-- yeah-- to dig the ditches. Trenches-- we dug trenches.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the barracks, in the-- Stutthof, and even in the-- the camps, the tents, who was in charge of the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Stutthof they had-- it were-- they were called kapos. They were in charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which are?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of Hungarian Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the kapos were who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews-- but also with-- with-- with Germans, I mean, Germans-- no Lithuanians anymore. And in--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the labor camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: --at labor camps, we had Germans-- Germans-- German soldiers, German police. They were watching us. But again, we were very happy to leave Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother was very afraid. She had this sixth sense and luck that-- can you imagine? That again-- [LAUGHS] Did you fall ill at all--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --during this period--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was OK too. But we were-- by the end of '44, we were. Because don't forget. We had to march from every camp to the other one, we marched. Now my mother says, and she's 100% right, that she did save my life. Because in-- during the marches, she kept on pushing me, not to fall down. If you fell down, you were finished. And she says she remembers-- I don't remember as well-- mothers cuddling their children, their daughters-- not children. They weren't children-- like daughters-- cuddling their daughters, and not being very strong emotionally to push them on. My mother pushed me on. And she also tells of a story. Maybe she'll be able to tell it to you herself-- taking my shoes and putting in her bosom to warm them up. But she kept on pushing. She was very short, but a dynamite. In-- in Russian they used to call her [NON-ENGLISH]. She was always in charge-- always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any babies born in these camps to your knowledge? Your mother being a nurse, she perhaps would have known about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe in Stutthof-- maybe in Stutthof-- not in the labor camps, because they wouldn't take you. You would have been killed-- maybe in Stutthof. In the ghetto there was a lot of babies born in the ghetto, of course. But I don't remember about Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're talking about camps--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the cold months,

speaker: interviewee
text: And the cold months.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and this work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the work, and people dying. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness acts of random killings?

speaker: interviewee
text: If-- what I witnessed, if you were left, if you were weak, you were left. You were just kicked and left.
